channel.title: Quest Design on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.
channel.description: Recent content in Quest Design on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.

item.title: Eight Level Design Guidelines
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about eight level design guidelines.

I don’t talk about level design much on this channel. I do have a level design playlist, which this will go on. And the reason for that is: like art, I don’t do level design very much.

However, as a game director, you are frequently expected to give guidelines to everyone on your team, not just people whose jobs you’ve done before. This is what happened in this one. I was asked to give some guidelines for what I wanted as level design. I think this was for The Outer Worlds, but it was based on something I had written back at uh Carbine for Wildstar. I wanted to make a top 10 list. I always like doing things in like, you know, top five or top 10, but I only came up with eight. So these are the eight you’re going to get. And I know that sounds disappointing, but if you have played any of my games, you know I’m not afraid to disappoint.

item.title: Quest Types
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to talk about quest types.

What I mean by that is, in a role-playing game, you can often have a lot of different types of quests, and people talk about this a lot. Oh, I went on a kill quest and I went on a collect quest, or I went on a fetch quest. These are all different types of quests that are usually implemented in a role-playing game, and for different reasons. I’m going to talk about why, but I I thought about this because I recently made a video just about escort quests, which is one type of quest type. And I really doubled down on issues that arise from escort quests And I realized — even though, okay, I’ll admit, I’m not a huge fan of escort quests, but I realized — that a lot of these things apply to the other quest types. So I wanted to talk about them and explain, first of all, why all these fixes people were offering — they’re possible, but why they may not have.

item.title: Escort Quests
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about escort quests.

A while back I did a video called RPG features. Nobody asked for and I included escort quests on that video. Why? I’ve never seen a review asking for more escort quests ever. Not once I’ve seen reviews going: “I wish there was more combat in here. I wish the combat was better. I wish there was more opportunity to do stealth quests. I wish there was more dialogue with NPCs. But not once have I seen a review going: “Do you know what This RPG needs?

item.title: Botched Quests And Player Freedom
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about botched quests and player Freedom.

This comes from a question from Nick Berg, one who asked: can you share your ideas on botched, failed quests? Don’t these limit the freedom of making your own story and RPGs? I actually think it expands your freedom. So I think that’s a good question and I want to explain my answer. I think Nick is referring to a video I did on Quest implementation and I will link it below.

item.title: Dynamic Quest Design
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Dynamic Quest Design.

This is a question from Anubis 111, who asked on my tabletop roleplaying vid. I would love to hear how you’d approach a radiant Quest system. You’ve mentioned it in passing, but your games have never made big use of them. How do you feel about them in general, and if you had to implement such a system, how would you bring it in line with Tim’s style of game design?

item.title: Shopping Cart Theory
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about shopping cart Theory.

This is for narrative game narrative designers or budding game narrative designers out there. It’s just something for you to think about. It’s a very interesting way to think about and frame morality, both in real life and in video games. The shopping cart theory is a very simple philosophical situation that illustrates Ates people’s moralities in a very Stark, clear, simple, clean way. So of course, I love it because it’s simple, but it makes you think things and you realize quickly things aren’t as simple and straightforward as you might think. So what is shopping cart Theory? Basically what this is.

item.title: The Horizontal Slice
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the horizontal slice.

I will explain what I mean by that. I’m actually going to answer a question from John flyer 4873, who asks: do you have a particular balance of quest content that you try to reach? Is there a process that goes into determining how long SL complex the main quest is versus how many side quests there are and where they are in your world? Let me just say that I didn’t used to have any process for that, it was just feel it as you go, and you can kind of see that in Fallout and Arcanum, where there are big chunks of the world that don’t have anything in them, um, there are, there are clusters of places where there are a lot more quests than other places. Um, Temple, of course, was made um off a module and, even though we had trouble with some areas were too dense.

item.title: Companions
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about companions.

Got a lot of questions about Companions and how I feel like they should be used in RPGs and I don’t have a simple answer, which is why I put off answering this for such a long time, and I’ve gotten so many qu different questions from different people that I will try to cover them all in this one video. So it’s. It covers what — whether or not I think companions should be in games, and how many there should be and are they controllable, and what’s their involvement in the quest line, and blah. So let’s just dive in. There’s a lot of topics here. Companions are a nuanced topic, so, companions, let’s start with whether or not they’re controllable. In general, in isometric games, I lean towards them being controllable — that once they enter the party, yes, you control them.

item.title: Quest Markers
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about Quest markers.

Specifically, I want to answer a question from Wes 9353, who asks a question about Quest markers. What do you prefer: pointing at the quest item or Quest enemy even if the enem is invisible, or a quest marker that turns into a search radius, or no Quest marker? Figure it out through dialogue or environmental storytelling?

item.title: Multiple Quest Solutions
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about multiple Quest Solutions and I want to use an example from Fallout that I used in my talk at the reboot conference in 2017.

So that is rescuing Tandy from the Raiders. So this is the map. It happens on very simple map. I should point out one building couple tents — sorry, it’s shown here — after killing some guards or some Raiders.

item.title: Quest Implementation
item.description:
Hey everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about Quest implementation.

Christy tenay 6130 — I hope I got that name right — asks: hi, Tim, can you please explain more about how a quest system is created in code? Do you use a database or just classes? There are variables only or entire records. And how do you change the quest dates and do branching depending on the various State paths provided and chosen by the players?

item.title: Everything I learned from Disneyland
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to do something a little different.

All the other videos have been me talking just off the top of my head, but several people have asked in comments about what Inspirations I have for game design and if any of those Inspirations have come not from games or books or movies, but just like everyday activities. And I realized not only do I have just such an inspiration, but I’ve actually talked about this professionally twice, once to the area Design Group I had at carbine, and then again just a couple years ago to, uh, the team on The Outer Worlds 2.. So I wanted to talk to you today about what would be called the top 10 area design roles, or what I instead called Everything I know about RPGs. I learned it, so this could be a little long.
